planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly into sandy, well-drained coastal soil; scarify the hard seed coat with sandpaper or nick with a file before sowing, as sea pea has an exceptionally tough seed coat that delays germination without mechanical assistance.

directions: Scarify seed coats by rubbing on sandpaper or nicking with a file, then sow directly into sandy, sharply drained soil in full sun; sea pea is a sprawling perennial native to coastal beaches, dunes, and shore margins throughout the Northern Hemisphere, where it stabilizes sandy substrates with deep, spreading roots and fixes atmospheric nitrogen with rhizobial bacteria. The bicolor purple and pink flowers appear in early summer and are followed by short, curved seed pods. It tolerates salt spray, drought, and nutrient-poor sand once established, but resents transplanting due to its deep root system — sow in situ or use deep biodegradable pots to start seedlings. In gardens it performs best in lean, gritty soils; rich soils promote lush foliage at the expense of flowering.

planting.note: Sow direct in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or in fall for spring germination; scarify or soak seed 24 hours before sowing to soften the hard seed coat and improve germination speed.

directions: Sow scarified or soaked seed 1 inch deep in spring into lean, well-drained soil in full sun, providing a trellis, fence, or shrub margin for the tendrils to grip; flat pea is a tough European perennial vine that climbs to 4–6 feet by summer and produces clusters of small pink and magenta-crimson flowers with distinctive winged stems from which the common name derives. Unlike the annual sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus), it has no fragrance, but is far more persistent — once established it returns reliably each spring and spreads gradually by seed. It is drought-tolerant and will grow in poor, thin soils where little else climbs, making it valuable on dry banks, hedgerow margins, and informal screens. Cut stems back to the ground in late fall to remove old growth and prevent untidy sprawl.

planting.note: Sow seed direct in early spring after scarifying or soaking overnight; alternatively, sow in fall and allow the seed to cold-stratify naturally over winter — this Mediterranean perennial establishes most reliably from seed sown in situ, as its spreading rhizomes resent disturbance once established.

directions: Sow scarified or overnight-soaked seed 1 inch deep into well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun, with a trellis, wire, or shrub for support; two-flowered everlasting pea is considered the most ornamental of the perennial peas, bearing pairs of exceptionally large, richly colored deep-pink to magenta flowers on each stem from early to midsummer — the blooms are substantially larger than those of the common everlasting pea (Lathyrus latifolius) and make a striking display on arches, obelisks, or through open shrubs. It spreads steadily by rhizomes and will naturalize freely in well-drained soils, forming sizeable clumps in time; site it where it can expand without crowding small neighbors. Like most perennial peas it is drought-tolerant once established and fixes its own nitrogen, performing best in lean rather than heavily enriched soils.

planting.note: Broadcast or drill seed in early spring 2–6 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall; inoculate with the correct Rhizobium leguminosarum biovar viciae inoculant before sowing for maximum nitrogen fixation, and mix with a support grass such as oats or rye to provide climbing structure and prevent lodging.

directions: Broadcast seed at 1–2 lb per 1,000 sq ft into a raked seedbed, or drill at 1/2 inch depth, ideally inoculated and mixed with a companion grass (oats, rye, or timothy) that gives the scrambling stems something to climb; tufted vetch is a widely naturalized perennial of meadows, hedgerows, and field margins across the Northern Hemisphere that produces long racemes of densely packed blue-purple flowers from late spring through summer, making it highly valuable to bees, bumblebees, and other long-tongued pollinators. As a nitrogen-fixing legume it can fix 100–150 lb of nitrogen per acre per year when well inoculated; it can be cut and incorporated into soil at early flowering for a green manure benefit, or left to bloom and seed for wildlife habitat and naturalized meadow plantings. Note that tufted vetch can spread aggressively in favorable conditions and is considered weedy in some regions — monitor spread and remove seed heads if naturalization is not desired.

planting.note: Sow direct 4–6 weeks before last frost in early spring; grass pea tolerates frost once established and can also be sown in fall in mild-winter climates (zones 7–10) for a winter-to-spring crop; inoculate with a broad-spectrum Rhizobium inoculant if growing in a new bed.

directions: Sow seed 1–2 inches deep in cool, well-drained soil in full sun, 4–6 weeks before last frost; grass pea is one of the most ancient cultivated grain legumes, domesticated in the Fertile Crescent and still grown today across South Asia, Ethiopia, and the Mediterranean as a drought-resistant crop that produces nutritious, starchy seeds used whole, split, or ground into flour. It fixes atmospheric nitrogen and performs remarkably well in thin, poor, drought-stressed soils where other legumes fail — making it a resilient choice for dry-summer gardens and marginal land. Provide light support for the scrambling stems and harvest pods when fully dry on the vine. Important safety note: prolonged consumption of large quantities of grass pea seeds as a dietary staple without adequate protein diversity can cause lathyrism, a neurological condition — consume in normal dietary variety and not as a sole food source.
